summary: O'Neil celebrates the progress of the tampon tax abolition movement, highlighting her friend Laura Strausfeld's successful advocacy work featured in a Washington Post op-ed. The post reflects on O'Neil's own involvement as a plaintiff in a New York lawsuit against the tampon tax years earlier. She explains how Strausfeld strategically coined the clinical term 'menstrual discharge collection device' (MDCD) to help male legislators understand these products' necessity. The movement is gaining momentum, with Texas recently eliminating its tampon tax.
key_points: The tampon tax abolition movement has achieved significant success, with momentum building nationwide
Strategic reframing through clinical terminology ('MDCD') proved effective in getting male legislators to understand menstrual products' necessity
Texas eliminating its tampon tax signals broader political acceptance of the issue
Sustained advocacy over many years (since at least 2016) is finally paying off
money_quotes: Holy shit I look amazing holding tampons

It's a hilarious description ('womansplaining') of why a ridiculous term, namely 'menstrual discharge collection device,' or MDCD, was coined for one purpose: to get men in power to understand that tampons and other menstrual products are things that prevent women from bleeding all over subway cars.
They somehow didn't get that until the name was changed! No kidding!
Texas just got rid of theirs, and that bodes very well.
key_insight: Sometimes achieving policy change requires reframing basic concepts in jarring clinical terms to force those in power to actually confront what they're legislating.
